intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The Deep Agents CLI is a local developer command-line tool that scaffolds, bundles, and ships a deepagents agent project to a managed deployment platform (LangSmith Deployments / LangGraph Platform). It exposes exactly three subcommands — init (scaffold a starter project), dev (bundle and serve locally), and deploy (bundle and push to the platform) — and talks to the platform plus its hub and tracing services using the developer's own credentials. Its central promise is that the deployed bundle faithfully and only reflects the developer's reviewed project: bundling only declared sources, never embedding secrets into payloads that should not carry them, and requiring explicit operator confirmation before any deploy that would leave the deployed API open without authentication.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python 3.11+ argparse CLI (main.py) dispatching to a deploy pipeline under deepagents_cli/deploy/ — commands.py (init/dev/deploy handlers), bundler.py (assembles the build directory), config.py (parses/validates deepagents.toml), templates.py (string templates for the generated deploy_graph.py, auth.py, pyproject.toml), and context_hub.py (LangSmith Hub backend). The bundler reads a canonical project layout (deepagents.toml, AGENTS.md, optional mcp.json, .env, skills/, user/, subagents/) and writes a build directory consumed by a shelled-out langgraph CLI. It ships a pre-built React frontend with three auth modes (supabase, clerk, anonymous). Notable: deploy performs a post-deploy _auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub call that provisions a recurring cron issues-agent on the platform for hub-backed deploys; _validate_mcp_for_deploy rejects stdio transports but does not enforce TLS on http/sse URLs; the in-repo THREAT_MODEL.md is self-flagged as stale.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy-implementation divergence concentrated on the CLI's two explicit safety promises — the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate and the "only declared sources" guarantee. The anonymous-auth confirmation prompt in _deploy is gated on frontend.enabled AND auth.provider == "anonymous", yet bundler.py generates the permissive auth.py handler whenever any [auth] provider is set — so a project with provider = "anonymous" but no [frontend] section ships an open API with no warning and no confirmation.

Separately, _auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub provisions a recurring cron-scheduled issues-agent on the platform after a hub-backed deploy — an agent surface the developer never declared — and remote MCP servers carried into the bundle are neither TLS-enforced nor version/integrity-pinned, both of which the remit explicitly requires. The supporting THREAT_MODEL.md is self-admittedly stale, so these gaps are also undocumented.

rules.rule_text: The CLI MUST bundle only from the project's declared sources — its configuration, system prompt, skills, declared MCP servers, and declared subagents — and MUST NOT pull in undeclared content.
A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.
Project configuration MUST be validated before bundling, and a bundle that fails validation MUST NOT be deployed.

Credentials — deployment-platform keys, model-provider API keys, hub and tracing tokens, frontend auth secrets — MUST NOT be written into the project, committed to version control, logged, or printed.
Secret material MUST NOT be embedded into bundle artifacts that are not meant to carry it — environment files travel as environment files, never folded into the seeded-memory or skills payload.
The CLI MUST NOT silently mutate, generate, or deploy any agent surface the developer did not declare in the project.
Overwriting an existing project folder or its files during scaffolding MUST require an explicit operator opt-in.
The CLI operates under direct developer supervision as a one-shot command; it MUST NOT run as an unattended background service.
Before a deploy, the CLI MUST present the operator a clear summary of what the bundle contains and where it will be shipped — enough for the developer to recognise the surface being deployed.
A dry run MUST generate the deployment artifacts without shipping them or contacting the deployment platform's mutating endpoints.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, pinned to compatible ranges, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

The deployment bundle's own dependency set MUST be derived only from the project's declared model provider, declared MCP usage, declared sandbox provider, and declared auth provider — never hand-edited into the bundle out of band.
Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships — confirming that the bundler carries only declared sources, that secrets never land in the seeded payload, and that the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate genuinely fires.

findings.summary: The unauthenticated-API confirmation gate fires only when [frontend].enabled is set, so an anonymous-auth project with no frontend ships an open API silently.
findings.description: The remit names the open-API confirmation prompt as one of the CLI's core protections. In _deploy, the is_anonymous guard requires both a bundled frontend and the anonymous auth provider, but the permissive auth.py is generated for any [auth] provider — decoupling the open-API condition from the gate that is supposed to protect it.
findings.policy_rule_text: A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.

evidence.snippet: is_anonymous requires `config.frontend is not None and config.frontend.enabled` AND `config.auth.provider == "anonymous"`; the warning + confirm block is gated on this compound condition.

auth.py is generated whenever `auth_provider is not None`; the anonymous handler returns {"identity": "anonymous"} for every request, so anonymous auth opens the API regardless of whether a frontend is bundled.
AUTH_BLOCK_ANONYMOUS docstring: overrides LangSmith Cloud's default x-api-key requirement; anyone with the deploy URL can call the API directly via curl.

recommended_actions: In commands.py, key the anonymous warning and confirmation prompt on config.auth is not None and config.auth.provider == "anonymous" alone — drop the frontend.enabled precondition so every anonymous deploy is gated.

findings.summary: deploy auto-provisions a recurring cron-scheduled issues-agent on the platform for hub-backed deploys — an agent surface the developer never declared.
findings.description: After a hub-backed deploy, _auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub POSTs an issues-agent configuration with a six-hourly cron schedule and heavy/light models to the platform. Because hub is the default memories backend, this fires for typical deploys with no operator prompt and no mention in the pre-deploy bundle summary.
findings.policy_rule_text: The CLI MUST NOT silently mutate, generate, or deploy any agent surface the developer did not declare in the project.

evidence.snippet: _deploy calls _auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub(config) after _run_langgraph_deploy, with no operator prompt.

_upsert_issues_board_config POSTs create_payload with "cron_schedule": "0 */6 * * *" and heavy_model/light_model to the platform issues-agent endpoint — provisioning a background agent that runs every 6 hours.
_auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub runs unconditionally whenever config.memories.backend == "hub", which is the default backend in MemoriesConfig.

recommended_actions: Gate _auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub behind an explicit project declaration or an interactive operator opt-in.
Include the recurring issues-agent in the print_bundle_summary output so the operator sees the surface before the deploy proceeds.

findings.summary: _validate_mcp_for_deploy rejects stdio MCP transports but never checks that http/sse server URLs use TLS, so a plaintext http:// MCP server passes validation into the bundle.
findings.description: The remit requires that remote MCP servers carried into the bundle be reached over TLS and have their transport validated before shipping. The validator only rejects the stdio transport; it never inspects the URL scheme, so a non-TLS endpoint is bundled and connected to at runtime.
findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.

evidence.snippet: _validate_mcp_for_deploy only flags transport == "stdio"; it does not inspect the url scheme for http vs https.
Generated _load_mcp_tools builds connections for any transport in ("http", "sse") using cfg["url"] verbatim, with no scheme check.

recommended_actions: In _validate_mcp_for_deploy, add a validation error for any http/sse server whose url does not start with https://, so non-TLS MCP endpoints fail validation before the bundle ships.

findings.summary: Remote MCP servers carried into the deployment bundle are copied verbatim with no version pin or integrity check, so the deployed agent loads whatever the URL serves at runtime.
findings.description: The remit's Known Good Baseline requires declared remote MCP servers to be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version. The bundler copies the project mcp.json straight through, and the generated runtime loader fetches tool definitions live from the URL on each call — leaving the deployed agent exposed to silent tool-definition changes (rug pull) and tool poisoning.

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned

findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

evidence.snippet: bundle() copies the project mcp.json straight to _mcp.json (shutil.copy2) with no pinning step.
_load_mcp_tools calls MultiServerMCPClient(connections).get_tools() at runtime against the live URL; tool definitions are whatever the server returns each call.
The repo's own MCP config (docs-langchain, reference-langchain) is HTTP-transport with bare URLs and no integrity metadata, illustrating the unpinned shape the bundler accepts.

recommended_actions: Require an integrity reference — a pinned server version, an expected tool-manifest hash, or a signature — for each remote MCP server, and reject or warn at bundle time when one is absent.

findings.summary: The generated bundle's pyproject.toml pins deepagents==0.5.3 while the CLI itself requires deepagents>=0.6.1, shipping deployed agents on an SDK six minor versions behind the tested baseline.
findings.description: The bundler's PYPROJECT_TEMPLATE hard-codes an SDK pin that has drifted away from the SDK version the CLI is built and tested against. Deployed agents therefore run a different, older SDK than the bundler was validated with.

evidence.snippet: PYPROJECT_TEMPLATE hard-codes "deepagents==0.5.3" in the generated bundle's dependency list.
The CLI package itself depends on "deepagents>=0.6.1"; the bundle is built and tested against 0.6.x but deploys 0.5.3.

recommended_actions: Bump the deepagents== pin in PYPROJECT_TEMPLATE to match the SDK version the CLI is built against.
Add a CI check that keeps the template pin and the CLI's SDK floor in lock-step (the repo already has check_lockfiles.yml).

findings.summary: libs/cli/THREAT_MODEL.md is self-flagged as stale — it documents the pre-0.1.0 REPL surface that no longer ships in this package.
findings.description: The remit asks the project to keep its threat model current with what the package actually ships. The committed threat model carries a banner admitting it predates the 0.1.0 split and still scopes modules — Textual app, non_interactive.py, MCP, sandbox bootstrap — that are no longer part of deepagents-cli.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships — confirming that the bundler carries only declared sources, that secrets never land in the seeded payload, and that the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate genuinely fires.

evidence.snippet: Banner: this document was generated before the 0.1.0 split; the sections covering the Textual app, non_interactive.py, MCP, sandbox bootstrap, sessions, server subprocess management, hooks, and skills no longer apply to this package.

The In Scope list still names app.py, non_interactive.py, agent.py, tools.py, mcp_tools.py — modules absent from the current deepagents_cli/ tree (now only main.py, config.py, model_config.py, _version.py, deploy/).

recommended_actions: Regenerate THREAT_MODEL.md against the current deepagents_cli.deploy surface — bundler, templates, hub seeding, the anonymous-auth gate, and MCP TLS handling — so the published model matches what the package ships.

findings.summary: The deploy CLI installs no logging handlers, so diagnostics and bundle-composition records are invisible and no durable record of what was deployed is kept.
findings.description: The CLI does not configure logging handlers, so logger.warning / logger.info calls — including the record of what memories, skills, and user files were bundled, and hub-seed errors — are dropped. There is no structured, durable record of a deploy.

evidence.snippet: _stderr helper comment: the CLI does not configure logging handlers by default, so logger.warning output is invisible to end users.
Bundle composition (memories, skills, user_memories counts) is recorded only via logger.info, which is dropped with no handler installed.

recommended_actions: Write a structured deploy record — timestamp, bundle contents, destination, MCP servers, auth mode — to a file or the platform, so each deploy leaves an auditable trail.

positives.title: Strict config schema rejects unknown sections and keys
positives.description: _parse_config and _parse_subagent_config reject any unknown top-level section or unknown [agent]/[sandbox]/[auth]/[memories]/[frontend] key, preventing silent acceptance of typo'd or stale configuration.

positives.title: Secrets excluded from the seed payload by design
positives.description: _build_seed reads only AGENTS.md, skills/, user/, and subagents/ and never folds .env into _seed.json; .env is copied separately as an env file, so secrets do not land in the seeded-memory payload.

positives.title: Scaffolding overwrite guard
positives.description: _init_project refuses to write into an existing project directory unless the operator passes --force, satisfying the explicit-opt-in requirement for overwriting project files.

positives.title: Committed pinned lockfile plus supply-chain CI
positives.description: uv.lock is committed and pinned, and .github/ carries dependabot.yml, check_lockfiles.yml, and check_extras_sync.yml — the dependency baseline is version-controlled and continuously checked.

positives.title: Substantial deploy-pipeline test suite
positives.description: Unit and integration tests cover the bundler, config validation, context hub, and frontend bundle, including the anonymous-auth abort-on-no and abort-on-EOF paths.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were found in the workspace; the CLI installs no logging handlers by default (config.py) and writes no durable deploy record — see PRAX-2026-05-19-007 (Monitor Continuously).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Deep Agents CLI sits at Partial maturity. It has a coherent, narrowly-scoped design — three subcommands, strict config parsing, a committed lockfile, and a real test suite — and several of its safety mechanisms (config validation, supabase/clerk auth handlers, .env excluded from the seed payload) genuinely run. But the two protections the remit names as the CLI's core promise are only partially enforced: the open-API confirmation gate is mis-keyed to require a bundled frontend, and bundled remote MCP servers are neither TLS-validated nor integrity-pinned. With effectively no runtime logging and a self-admittedly stale threat model, divergences would be neither prevented at deploy time nor detected afterward.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The CLI is tightly scoped to three subcommands and a bare invocation deliberately redirects to deepagents-code and exits non-zero (main.py); deepagents.toml parsing rejects unknown sections and keys. The one domain leak is _auto_wire_issues_board_if_hub provisioning a recurring platform agent the project never declared.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The CLI is a one-shot bundler with no LLM context of its own; _build_seed reads only declared project sources and explicitly excludes .env from the seed payload. It does not validate the content of the AGENTS.md and skills it bundles, but that content is operator-authored.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Config validation runs before bundling and the deploy stops on errors (config.validate), and the supabase/clerk auth handlers are real, but the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate is mis-keyed to require [frontend].enabled, MCP http/sse URLs are not TLS-checked, and bundled MCP servers are unpinned — the agent's two stated protections are not fully enforced in code.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A committed pinned uv.lock exists and .github/ carries dependabot.yml, check_lockfiles.yml, and check_extras_sync.yml, but the generated bundle's pyproject.toml pins deepagents==0.5.3 while the CLI requires deepagents>=0.6.1, and bundled remote MCP servers carry no integrity pin.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: There is a substantial unit and integration test suite for the deploy pipeline and a THREAT_MODEL.md exists, but the threat model is self-flagged as stale (pre-0.1.0 surface) and no adversarial test exercises the anonymous-without-frontend open-API path or MCP TLS — the testing did not drive the design of the safety gates.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The CLI configures no logging handlers by default (config.py comment confirms logger.warning output is invisible to users) and diagnostics go to stderr ad hoc; there is no structured, durable record of what was bundled or deployed.
